title: Reading the Waveform
description: Learn to read waveform displays to find drops, breakdowns, and mix points before you hear them.

steps.instruction: Watch: the waveform display shows dense peaks where there is heavy audio — kick drums appear as sharp transients.
Watch: a breakdown appears as a quiet, thin section in the waveform. After it, the full section resumes — that is the drop.
Watch: the cue point is placed at the start of the dense section — right at the drop. This is your mix-out point.

phases.instruction: The waveform is a map of your track. Tall, dense mountains are the loud, energetic sections. Valleys and thin lines are the breakdowns. Before the valley ends, the mountain rises again — that is your drop. Reading this map means you always know what is coming before you hear it.

steps.instruction: Load a track on Deck A. Look at the waveform display — can you see sections that are denser than others?
Look ahead in the waveform. Identify where the next thin section is — that is the upcoming breakdown.
After the breakdown, find where the waveform gets dense again — that is the upcoming drop. Set a cue point at that dense section.
Let the track play through. When the playhead reaches your cue — does the sound match what you predicted from the waveform?
Now load a second track on Deck B. Find a good mix-in point on Deck B's waveform — look for a dense start after a breakdown.
Set a cue point on Deck B at that mix-in point. You now have both tracks mapped — ready to transition at the right visual moment.
